title: Java Multithreading Basics
description: Master Java multithreading by implementing thread-safe operations using synchronized blocks, Thread class, and Runnable interface. Learn to handle concurrent execution, race conditions, and shared resource access - critical skills for parallel test execution in automation frameworks.
instructions: Create a class `Counter` with a private integer field `count` initialized to 0
Implement a synchronized method `increment()` that increases the count by 1
Implement a synchronized method `getCount()` that returns the current count value
Create a `CounterTask` class that implements Runnable and increments the counter 1000 times
In the main method, create 10 threads using the CounterTask and start them all
Use join() to wait for all threads to complete before printing the final count
The final count should be exactly 10000 (10 threads × 1000 increments each)
learningObjectives: Understand thread creation using Thread class and Runnable interface
Master the synchronized keyword to prevent race conditions
Learn thread coordination using join() method
Understand shared resource access in multi-threaded environments

hints.content: Use the 'synchronized' keyword before the method return type to make methods thread-safe: 'public synchronized void increment()'. This ensures only one thread can execute the method at a time.
In CounterTask's run() method, use a for loop: 'for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++) { counter.increment(); }'. Create threads with 'new Thread(new CounterTask(counter))' and call start() on each thread.

Complete solution: Store threads in an array, start all threads in one loop, then use another loop to call join() on each thread. The join() method blocks until the thread completes, ensuring all 10,000 increments finish before printing the count.
